narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 63% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, based on patterns from thousands of past students. This model draws from key details in your profile, like your Year 1 school decile rank in the lowest group, your Year 3 rank just a bit higher in the second decile, your middle-family income bracket, LSAT score of 32, undergraduate GPA of 3.4, being a Black female planning full-time studies—compared to averages where most students rank around the middle or higher in school deciles, score about 37 on the LSAT, and come from varied but often higher-income backgrounds with White students making up the large majority. Your stronger-than-average undergrad GPA stands out positively, but it wasn't enough to offset other signals. The model's decision was most heavily swayed by your Year 3 decile rank, which strongly nudged the prediction toward expecting a bar failure since it's well below typical mid-range performers. Next in influence was your LSAT score, also pushing toward failure as it's noticeably lower than the average law student's. Your Year 1 decile rank followed closely, reinforcing that early low performance as a concern. Family income in the middle bracket had a moderate pull in the same direction, while your undergrad GPA, racial background, gender, and full-time status each played smaller roles, all slightly tilting toward the riskier outcome. Overall, these factors ranked in that order of impact, with school deciles and LSAT leading the way in signaling higher bar failure risk compared to peers who typically pass. It's tough to hear, especially since your GPA shows real academic capability. To boost your future bar exam odds, focus on what you can control: dedicate your full-time status to intensive bar prep courses, practice tests mimicking the LSAT's reasoning style to build those skills, and seek mentorship from successful bar takers from similar backgrounds to address early-grade patterns. With targeted effort, students like you have turned similar profiles around—keep pushing, and reapply once you've strengthened these areas. We're rooting for your success.
